papers.title: RdRpCATCH: a unified resource for RNA virus discovery using viral RNA-dependent RNA polymerase profile Hidden Markov models.
papers.abstract: Recent advances in large-scale sequence mining have expanded our knowledge of RNA virus diversity. Most genome mining approaches for detecting RNA viruses rely on identifying the conserved RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp) by scanning sequencing datasets with specialized profile Hidden Markov Models (pHMMs). Recently, several new pHMM databases for RdRp detection have been released, each following distinct design principles. However, their relative performance remains unclear, and their accessibility to users without advanced computational expertise is limited. Here, we introduce the RdRp Co
papers.summary: This paper presents RdRpCATCH, a unified resource that consolidates multiple profile Hidden Markov Model databases for detecting RNA virus RNA-dependent RNA polymerase (RdRp). The tool improves accessibility for non-expert users and provides a benchmark for comparing the performance of different RdRp detection approaches. This resource will facilitate broader RNA virus discovery from sequencing data.

papers.title: DiffMethylTools: a toolbox for the detection, annotation, and visualization of differential methylation.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation is a fundamental epigenetic mechanism, and its significant changes (i.e., differential methylation) regulate gene expression, cell-type specification, and disease progression without altering the underlying DNA sequence. Differential methylation has traditionally been detected by statistical comparison of two groups using existing tools and has supported a wide range of downstream analyses in human disease studies. However, few toolboxes efficiently integrate robust detection, annotation, and visualization of differential methylation, and the reproducibility of detected signals
papers.summary: DiffMethylTools is a new software toolbox that integrates robust detection, annotation, and visualization of differential DNA methylation in a single, user-friendly platform. It aims to improve reproducibility and streamline downstream analyses for epigenetic studies of human disease.

papers.title: Ensaya: An ensemble age model for prediction of chronological age in adolescents and young adults.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation-based models for chronological age estimation have, for over a decade, been among the most important methods for age prediction in humans. While large-scale data have enabled the development of numerous models, their accuracy varies substantially depending on choice of machine learning algorithm, pre-processing pipeline, age range, tissue type, and population characteristics. Accurate age estimation in adolescents and young adults is particularly relevant in legal, investigational, and biomedical contexts. Here, we systematically evaluated blood-based DNA methylation age predic
papers.summary: This paper presents Ensaya, a new ensemble model that combines multiple machine learning algorithms to predict chronological age from blood DNA methylation data, specifically optimized for adolescents and young adults. The model improves age estimation accuracy in this critical age range, which has important applications in forensic, legal, and biomedical settings.

papers.title: Rapid Histone Post-Translational Modification Analysis Using Alternative Proteases and Tandem Mass Tags.
papers.abstract: Histone post-translational modifications (PTMs) alter chromatin dynamics and contribute to the regulation of gene expression in health and disease, yet mass spectrometry-based histone PTM analysis remains constrained by inefficient sample preparation workflows. Here, we develop RIPUP (Rapid Identification of histone PTMs in Underivatized Peptides), a streamlined multiprotease workflow that reduces sample preparation to hours while improving PTM coverage and quantitative accuracy. Systematic evaluation of Arg-C Ultra and a recombinant (r)-Chymotrypsin protease under varied conditions, including
papers.summary: Researchers developed RIPUP, a faster and more efficient method for analyzing histone modifications using alternative proteases and tandem mass tags. This workflow reduces sample preparation time from days to hours while improving the detection and quantification of these important epigenetic marks.

papers.title: High-fidelity rare structural variant detection with HiFiRE3 reduced representation via restriction enzyme ends.
papers.abstract: High-fidelity detection of rare structural variants (SVs) remains challenging because library preparation and sequencing techniques generate artifactual junctions that obscure true single-molecule events. Here, we present HiFiRe3, an error-minimized sequencing framework that combines artifact-aware library design with error suppression and correction strategies to enable rare SV detection and frequency assessment across long and short-read sequencing platforms. We first systematically characterized major classes of SV artifacts, including chimeric PCR products, intermolecular ligation, sequenc
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: HiFiRE3 is a new sequencing method that reduces errors in detecting rare structural variants (SVs) by minimizing artifacts from library preparation and sequencing. It combines artifact-aware design with error suppression to accurately identify true SVs across both long and short-read platforms.

papers.title: Programmable aging function-related mitochondrial DNA 5-methylcytosine (m5C) modification with a TALE-directed methyltransferase.
papers.abstract: Mitochondrial epigenetic editing offers a potential strategy for modulating disease-causing mitochondrial genes while leaving the underlying DNA sequence unchanged. In this study, we present MEE, a mitochondrial epigenetic editor consisting of mitochondrion-targeted TALE modules fused to Dnmt3A and Dnmt3L methyltransferases. MEE efficiently directed site-specific 5mC methylation within cellular mitochondrial DNA with low detectable off-target activity under the tested conditions. MEE-mediated methylation at the C12191 (H) site exceeded 53% and was associated with an approximately 95% reduction
papers.summary: Researchers developed a programmable tool called MEE that can add specific epigenetic marks (5-methylcytosine) to mitochondrial DNA without changing the underlying sequence. This tool, which fuses mitochondrial-targeted TALE proteins with DNA methyltransferases, achieved over 53% methylation at a target site and reduced gene expression by approximately 95% with low off-target effects. This represents a significant advance in mitochondrial epigenetic editing with potential applications for modulating disease-causing mitochondrial genes.

papers.title: Epigenetic dysregulation in osteonecrosis of the femoral head: a critical review of DNA methylation, histone modifications, and clinical translation.
papers.abstract: Osteonecrosis of the Femoral Head (ONFH) is a progressive osteoarticular disease caused by local ischemia, ultimately leading to femoral head collapse and loss of joint function. Epigenetics has emerged as a pivotal tool for unraveling the complex pathogenesis of ONFH, with DNA methylation and histone modifications playing central roles. These key epigenetic mechanisms critically regulate osteocyte function, bone remodeling, and angiogenesis. Aberrant DNA methylation silences osteogenic genes, e.g., runt-related transcription factor 2(RUNX2), bone morphogenetic protein 2(BMP2) and angiogenic f
papers.journal: Journal of orthopaedic surgery and research
papers.summary: This review examines how DNA methylation and histone modifications contribute to osteonecrosis of the femoral head (ONFH) by silencing key osteogenic and angiogenic genes. It highlights the potential for epigenetic markers to serve as diagnostic tools or therapeutic targets for this progressive joint disease.

papers.title: Fine mapping of PTSD GWAS reveals a role for amygdala Foxp2 in regulation of fear and threat responses.
papers.abstract: Post-traumatic Stress Disorder (PTSD) is a debilitating psychiatric condition caused by severe trauma exposure and characterized by ongoing dysregulation of fear processing, hyperarousal, and amygdala activation, but with limited effective treatments. Recent large-scale genome-wide association studies (GWAS) of PTSD have identified the transcription factor FOXP2 as a highly-significant, top putative risk gene. Both fine-mapping of the PTSD GWAS with amygdala-specific expression quantitative trait loci (eQTL) data, and transcriptome-wide association analyses, show that altered expression of FOX
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study refines PTSD GWAS results to identify FOXP2 as a key risk gene, showing that its altered expression in the amygdala affects fear and threat responses. The findings link genetic risk to specific brain mechanisms, potentially guiding new treatments.

papers.title: Defining Pseudo-Haplotype Analysis Reveals Multi-Gene Genetic Pattern Across BAF Chromatin Remodeling Complexes.
papers.abstract: BRG1-associated factor (BAF) is a crucial chromatin remodeling complex. Variants in genes encoding BAF complex components cause human diseases, including cancers and developmental disorders. However, the genetic diversity and variant co-occurrence patterns within BAF genes remain incompletely understood. It is feasible, though largely untested, that rare patterns of common variations could alter function similarly to rare deleterious variants. Further, there is no modern census of how often individual people simultaneously carry multiple rare and common variations, nor means for genomics pract
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study introduces a pseudo-haplotype analysis method to investigate co-occurrence patterns of genetic variants across BAF chromatin remodeling complex genes. The authors find that individuals often carry multiple rare and common variations simultaneously, suggesting that combined genetic patterns may contribute to disease risk beyond individual variants.

papers.title: Novel Drosophila cis -regulatory elements can be uncovered by footprinting transcription factor binding sites in ATAC-seq data.
papers.abstract: Developmental processes depend on carefully coordinated gene expression. Expression is modulated by the binding of transcription factors (TFs) to cis-regulatory elements (CREs), like enhancers and promoters. Many computational and experimental approaches have been developed to find CREs, particularly enhancers, in the genome, each with strengths and caveats. Given the increasing availability of ATAC-seq data and methods to find TF binding therein, we hypothesized that we could use TF footprinting tools to find clusters of TF binding events within accessible chromatin that may act as CREs. Usin
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study demonstrates that transcription factor footprinting in ATAC-seq data can identify clusters of transcription factor binding events that correspond to cis-regulatory elements (CREs) like enhancers and promoters in Drosophila. By leveraging widely available ATAC-seq data and footprinting tools, the authors provide a computational approach to uncover novel regulatory elements without requiring additional experiments.

papers.title: kmerRRR: A k-mer based tool for functional genomics in Repeat Rich Regions.
papers.abstract: Highly repetitive sequences pose problems for genome assembly and analysis. While advances in long-read sequencing technologies have helped reveal the organization of repetitive genomic sequences at unprecedented resolution, their functional characterization remains difficult because molecular assays that probe protein-DNA interactions and characterize expression often rely on short read sequencing. The repetitive nature of these regions poses major challenges for methods relying on sequence mapping, which is exacerbated for short reads. Repetitive genome regions often have low mappability, le
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: The paper presents kmerRRR, a new computational tool that uses k-mer based analysis to study functional genomics in repetitive regions of the genome, which are typically difficult to analyze with short-read sequencing due to low mappability. By avoiding traditional sequence mapping, kmerRRR enables functional characterization of protein-DNA interactions and expression in repeat-rich regions that are often overlooked.

papers.title: Enhanced plant bottom-up histone proteomics.
papers.abstract: The correct tools for characterization of histone proteoforms are essential for deciphering plant epigenetic mechanisms and their subsequent application in biotechnology. Insights into the epigenetic landscape of plant chromatin can be advanced using bottom-up proteomics. MS analysis of histone peptides relies on careful sample preparation, including chemical derivatization of amine groups prior to MS to improve their chromatographic behaviour during nanoHPLC separation. Characterizing histones in plant tissues remains especially challenging due to the presence of diverse, species-specific com
papers.summary: This paper presents improved methods for bottom-up proteomics to characterize histone proteoforms in plants, which is crucial for understanding plant epigenetic mechanisms. The authors highlight the challenges of analyzing histones in plant tissues due to species-specific diversity and emphasize the importance of careful sample preparation and chemical derivatization for mass spectrometry.

papers.title: Catalytic footprints of DNMT1 dynamics during mammalian cell state transitions.
papers.abstract: 5-methylcytosine is a reversible regulatory mark in mammalian genomes whose distribution varies across genetic loci, cell types, and disease conditions. Cytosine-5 methylation is brought about by three AdoMet-dependent DNA methyltransferases (DNMTs) with primary tasks in de novo (DNMT3A and DNMT3B) or maintenance (DNMT1) methylation. However, the precise roles and interplay between the three methylation "writers" remain obscure. To selectively track an individual DNMT, we derived a heterozygous mESC line with chromosomal alleles expressing the wild-type and a sterically engineered variant of D
papers.summary: This study introduces a method to selectively track the activity of the maintenance DNA methyltransferase DNMT1 in mammalian cells by engineering a sterically controlled variant. Using this approach in mouse embryonic stem cells, the authors reveal distinct catalytic footprints of DNMT1 during cell state transitions, providing new insights into how this enzyme contributes to dynamic DNA methylation patterns.

papers.title: Pharmacogenomics and Epigenetic Regulation Transforming Pediatric Precision Therapeutics.
papers.abstract: Pediatric drug therapy remains fundamentally challenged by profound interindividual variability driven by dynamic development, genetic, and environmental factors. Although dosing strategies based on age, body weight, or body surface area remain important starting points in pediatric pharmacotherapy, they may not fully capture ontogeny-dependent variability in drug disposition and response. Consequently, clinically relevant differences in efficacy and toxicity may still occur among children receiving similar weight-adjusted doses. Pharmacogenomics offers a promising framework for individualized
papers.summary: This paper discusses how pharmacogenomics and epigenetic regulation can improve pediatric precision medicine by accounting for interindividual variability in drug response due to developmental, genetic, and environmental factors. It highlights the limitations of current dosing strategies based on age and weight, and proposes that integrating genomic and epigenetic data could reduce differences in drug efficacy and toxicity among children.

papers.title: Identification of compounds that repress DUX4 expression in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy.
papers.abstract: Facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD) is caused by epigenetic dysregulation of the disease locus, leading to pathogenic misexpression of DUX4 in skeletal muscle. Thus, most FSHD therapeutic approaches target DUX4. Our previous study identified the chromatin remodeling factor BAZ1A (bromodomain adjacent to zinc finger domain protein 1A) as a promising target for therapeutic development. Here we used an artificial intelligence-based screening pipeline to identify molecules predicted to bind the BAZ1A bromodomain, and validated hit compounds using FSHD-specific assays in FSHD myocytes. On
papers.summary: This study used an AI-based screening pipeline to identify small molecules predicted to bind the BAZ1A bromodomain, a chromatin remodeling factor implicated in DUX4 misexpression in facioscapulohumeral muscular dystrophy (FSHD). The hit compounds were validated in FSHD myocytes, showing potential to repress DUX4 expression and offering a new therapeutic avenue for FSHD.

papers.title: Epigenetic compartmentalization of mitotic chromosomes by phase-separation-driven repulsion between WDR5 and the chromosomal passenger complex.
papers.abstract: Histone modifications recruit chromatin regulators, but how their spatial patterns are established on mitotic chromosomes remains unclear. Spatial separation between active chromatin and centromeric signaling is essential for accurate chromosome segregation, yet the physical principles linking epigenetic marks to mitotic protein organization are poorly defined. Here, we show that liquid-liquid phase separation (LLPS) of WDR5, a core component of H3K4 methyltransferase complexes, acts as a spatial organizer of mitotic chromosomes. WDR5 LLPS is driven by multivalent charged and hydrophobic inter
papers.summary: This study reveals that the protein WDR5 forms liquid-like droplets on mitotic chromosomes through phase separation, and this process physically repels the chromosomal passenger complex to create distinct active and centromeric chromatin zones. This spatial organization is essential for accurate chromosome segregation during cell division.

papers.title: Epigenetic lockdown of type I interferon sensing and signalling in human pluripotent cells.
papers.abstract: The Human Silencing Hub (HUSH) complex safeguards genome integrity in human somatic cells, repressing transposable elements and regulating type I interferon (IFN-I) induction. Here, we use depletion of MPP8 in human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) as a tool to investigate epigenetic control of the IFN-I system in early development. We confirmed that human iPSCs display an attenuated IFN-I pathway, whereas iPSC-derived neural progenitor cells (NPCs) respond robustly to IFN-I pathway agonists. We found that, in iPSCs, depletion of MPP8 was sufficient to induce expression of young LINE-1 e
papers.summary: This study shows that human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) have a suppressed type I interferon (IFN-I) response due to epigenetic silencing by the HUSH complex, and that depleting the MPP8 component of this complex can reactivate IFN-I signaling. In contrast, neural progenitor cells derived from iPSCs regain a robust IFN-I response, indicating that this epigenetic lockdown is specific to pluripotent cells. The findings reveal a mechanism by which early developmental cells avoid immune activation, with implications for understanding viral susceptibility and immune regulation in stem cells.

papers.title: Integrating epigenetic and metabolic indicators for non-invasive endometrial cancer triage: a machine learning approach.
papers.abstract: Abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB) is a primary symptom indicative of endometrial cancer (EC), yet its diagnosis still primarily relies on invasive biopsy. This study aimed to develop and validate a non-invasive, interpretable machine learning (ML) model integrating local epigenetic (exfoliated cell CDO1 methylation) and systemic metabolic indicators for EC triage.
papers.summary: This study developed a non-invasive machine learning model that combines epigenetic markers (CDO1 methylation from exfoliated cells) with systemic metabolic indicators to triage endometrial cancer in patients with abnormal uterine bleeding, potentially reducing the need for invasive biopsies. The model offers an interpretable approach for clinical decision-making.

papers.title: Integrative multi-omics quantitative trait loci prioritize CASP7 as a candidate protective gene for cataract.
papers.abstract: Cataracts are the leading cause of vision loss worldwide. Despite surgery being the only effective treatment, its economic burden highlights the necessity of exploring the pathogenesis of cataracts. In this study, we analyzed 4 large-scale GWAS (genome-wide association study) datasets for cataracts and performed SMR analysis along with heterogeneity in dependent instruments (HEIDI) testing to explore the effects of methylation, expression, and protein QTLs on cataracts. We further validated shared genetic variants through COLOC analysis. Additionally, we searched datasets related to cataracts
papers.summary: This study integrated multi-omics quantitative trait loci (QTLs) from large-scale GWAS datasets to identify CASP7 as a candidate protective gene for cataracts. By analyzing methylation, expression, and protein QTLs with SMR, HEIDI, and COLOC methods, the authors provide evidence that genetic variants influencing CASP7 may reduce cataract risk.

papers.title: In vitro assays, probiogenomic analysis, safety assessment and probiotic potential of Enterococcus faecium F25.
papers.abstract: The probiogenomic approach has gained considerable attention for elucidating the genetic basis of probiotic functionality. In this study, the probiotic potential of Enterococcus faecium F25 was comprehensively evaluated using an integrated strategy combining traditional biochemical characterization, in vitro functional assays, and whole-genome-based analyses. The in vitro assessments were conducted using plate-based assays, while genomic investigations were performed using publicly available bioinformatic tools and platforms. Both approaches consistently confirmed the strain's typical enteroco
papers.summary: This study evaluates the probiotic potential of Enterococcus faecium F25 using a combined approach of traditional lab tests and whole-genome analysis, confirming its safety and functional properties. The integrated strategy demonstrates how genomic tools can validate and extend findings from conventional biochemical assays for probiotic characterization.

papers.title: Panorama: A robust pangenome-based method for predicting and comparing biological systems across species.
papers.abstract: Over the last decade, the expansion in the number of available genomes has profoundly transformed the study of genetic diversity, evolution, and ecological adaptation in prokaryotes. However, traditional bioinformatic approaches based on the analysis of individual genomes are showing their limitations when faced with the sheer scale of the data. To overcome these constraints, the concept of pangenome has emerged, offering a comprehensive framework to capture the full genetic repertoire of a species. In this study, we present PANORAMA, an innovative pangenomic tool designed to exploit pangenome
papers.summary: PANORAMA is a new computational tool that uses pangenome data to predict and compare biological systems across prokaryotic species, overcoming limitations of traditional single-genome analysis. It provides a robust framework for capturing the full genetic repertoire of a species, enabling better study of genetic diversity, evolution, and ecological adaptation.

papers.title: Exploring life's hidden majority: microbial dark matter symposium highlights.
papers.abstract: The Microbial Dark Matter Symposium held on August 28-29, 2025, in Laguna Beach, Orange County, CA, convened a multidisciplinary group of scientists to address the vast unknowns in microbial life-from uncultured taxa and uncharacterized proteins to elusive viruses and spacefaring microbes. Set against a scenic coastal backdrop, the symposium highlighted advances in single-cell genomics, proximity ligation sequencing, and artificial intelligence-ready bioinformatics, while also probing the limits of microbial persistence, metabolism, and ecological distribution. Sessions explored microbial dark
papers.summary: This symposium report summarizes discussions on the vast unknowns in microbial life, including uncultured taxa, uncharacterized proteins, and elusive viruses. Advances in single-cell genomics, proximity ligation sequencing, and AI-ready bioinformatics were highlighted as key tools for exploring microbial dark matter.

papers.title: Application of plant viruses in biotechnology with relation to artificial intelligence, machine learning, and nanotechnology.
papers.abstract: Plant viruses, once viewed as harmful agricultural pathogens, are now powerful tools in biotechnology. Their nanoscale structure, self-assembly, and biocompatibility enable applications in agriculture, medicine, and environmental sustainability. They serve in gene delivery, genome editing, diagnostics, and nanomaterials for vaccines and drug delivery. Integration with AI, ML, and bioinformatics enhances virus discovery and prediction. Despite challenges, plant viruses are emerging as versatile, sustainable resources for global biotechnological innovations.
papers.summary: This review highlights how plant viruses, traditionally seen as agricultural threats, are being repurposed as versatile biotechnological tools due to their nanoscale structure and self-assembly properties. The paper discusses their applications in gene delivery, genome editing, diagnostics, and nanomaterials, and explores how AI and machine learning can enhance virus discovery and prediction. Despite ongoing challenges, plant viruses are emerging as sustainable resources for innovations in agriculture, medicine, and environmental science.

papers.title: Single-cell DNA methylation profiling: Technologies, computation, and applications in precision oncology.
papers.abstract: Cancer is an intrinsically heterogeneous disease characterized by distinct malignant subclones defined by specific genetic and epigenetic alterations, such as aberrant DNA methylation. Traditional bulk sequencing methods analyze large populations of cells in aggregate, yielding an averaged methylome signal that masks the rare but clinically significant epigenetic patterns driving tumor initiation, metastasis, and therapeutic resistance. The emergence of single-cell DNA methylation (scDNAme) sequencing has driven a paradigm shift in oncology by providing the resolution required to dissect this
papers.summary: This review discusses how single-cell DNA methylation sequencing technologies enable the dissection of epigenetic heterogeneity in tumors, revealing rare but clinically important methylation patterns that drive cancer progression and treatment resistance, which are masked by traditional bulk sequencing methods.

papers.title: NTM-DB: A Comprehensive Non-tuberculosis Mycobacteria Genomic Database.
papers.abstract: Non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM) are a major group of environmental bacteria, approximately one-third of which cause serious human infections, particularly respiratory diseases. The global rise in the prevalence and severity of NTM infections has posed a major public health challenge. While high-throughput sequencing has generated vast genomic data on NTM, there remains a lack of comprehensive resources for cross-species genomic analysis. To address these limitations, we developed a specialized database, the Non-tuberculosis Mycobacteria Genomic Database (NTM-DB), tailored for NTM researcher
papers.summary: Researchers created NTM-DB, a specialized genomic database for non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM), which are environmental bacteria causing serious human infections. This resource fills a gap by providing a comprehensive platform for cross-species genomic analysis of NTM, addressing the lack of such tools despite abundant sequencing data.

papers.title: Cross-definition GWAS of IBS in 2.8 million individuals reveals cardiometabolic and triglyceride-linked mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is a complex disorder of gut-brain interaction, with heterogeneous symptoms, no available biomarkers and limited pathogenetic insight.
papers.summary: A large-scale genetic study of 2.8 million individuals identified shared genetic factors between irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and cardiometabolic traits, particularly triglyceride levels, suggesting new biological mechanisms linking gut-brain interactions with metabolic health. The findings provide insights into IBS pathogenesis and potential therapeutic targets.

papers.title: GenART: Reading the genome's language with adaptive "words".
papers.abstract: Tokenization is both a prerequisite and a central challenge for genomic language models, due to the inherent difficulty of delineating meaningful segments within continuous DNA sequences. We present GenART, a genomic language model framework that dynamically segments DNA into variable-length words directly from raw sequences without manual annotation. Its key adaptive tokenization module infers biologically meaningful boundaries from multi-scale contextual signals during pretraining. GenART demonstrates competitive performance across diverse tasks, outperforming leading approaches. Word bounda
papers.summary: GenART introduces a new way to tokenize DNA sequences for genomic language models by dynamically learning variable-length 'words' directly from raw sequence data, rather than relying on fixed or manually defined segments. This adaptive approach allows the model to infer biologically meaningful boundaries from context, leading to improved performance across multiple genomic tasks compared to existing methods.

papers.title: Context-aware simulation enables systematic optimization of long-read mapping parameters.
papers.abstract: Long-read mapping performance is critical for downstream genomic analyses but remains sensitive to parameter selection. We present CycSim, a context-aware long-read simulator that learns sequence-context-dependent error profiles from empirical data and generates realistic simulated reads. CycSim more faithfully recapitulated real long-read characteristics than existing simulators, providing a high-fidelity simulation framework with known ground truth. Using this framework, we identified a Cyclone-specific parameter set that achieved 2.78-fold faster mapping than an ONT-oriented baseline while
papers.summary: CycSim is a new simulator that learns realistic error patterns from real long-read sequencing data, producing more accurate simulated reads than existing tools. Using CycSim, researchers optimized mapping parameters for Cyclone software, achieving nearly 3x faster mapping without sacrificing accuracy. This provides a valuable framework for systematically tuning long-read analysis pipelines.

papers.title: TNEAtlas: A Pan-cancer Database to Identify and Characterize Transcribed Non-coding Elements.
papers.abstract: Advances in precision oncology have underscored the need to move beyond regulatory frameworks centered on protein-coding regions and better understand regulatory mechanisms within the non-coding genome. To systematically characterize transcribed non-coding elements (TNEs) in cancer, we developed an automated computational framework that integrates 130 RNA-seq datasets from 26 cancer types and 16 human tissues and identifies more than 2 million intergenic TNEs. In parallel, we established the first pan-cancer TNE database, TNEAtlas, which annotates TNEs with epigenetic signatures and functional
papers.summary: The authors developed a computational framework to identify over 2 million transcribed non-coding elements (TNEs) across 26 cancer types from 130 RNA-seq datasets, and created the first pan-cancer database (TNEAtlas) that annotates these elements with epigenetic and functional information. This resource enables systematic characterization of the non-coding genome in cancer, moving beyond traditional protein-coding focused analyses.

papers.title: AI-driven neoantigen identification: a comprehensive review from somatic variant calling to T cell recognition.
papers.abstract: Neoantigens-tumor-specific peptides generated by somatic mutations-are central targets of effective anticancer T cell immunity and underpin the clinical success of immune checkpoint blockade and personalized cancer vaccines. Advances in high-throughput sequencing, immunopeptidomics, and artificial intelligence (AI) have transformed neoantigen discovery from tailored experimental workflows into scalable, computational pipelines. However, accurately identifying the small subset of tumor mutations that yield processed, presented, and immunogenic epitopes remains a major bottleneck.
papers.summary: This review examines how AI and high-throughput technologies have transformed neoantigen discovery from experimental methods to scalable computational pipelines. It highlights that accurately identifying which tumor mutations produce immunogenic epitopes remains a key challenge for personalized cancer vaccines and immune checkpoint therapy.

papers.title: High-accuracy SNV calling for bacterial isolates using deep learning with AccuSNV.
papers.abstract: Accurate detection of mutations within bacterial species is critical for fundamental studies of microbial evolution, reconstructing transmission events, and identifying antimicrobial resistance mutations. While many tools have been developed to identify single nucleotide variants (SNVs) from whole-genome sequencing, they often suffer from high false positive rates due to the complexity of bacterial genomes and the need for different filtering cutoffs across sample types and sequencing depths. As datasets increase in size, the manual filtering required for high accuracy presents a significant o
papers.summary: AccuSNV is a new deep learning tool that improves the accuracy of detecting single nucleotide variants (SNVs) in bacterial genomes, reducing false positives that plague existing methods. It eliminates the need for manual filtering by automatically adapting to different sequencing depths and sample types, making it suitable for large-scale bacterial genomics studies.

papers.title: OMIDIENT: Multiomics Integration for Cancer by Dirichlet Auto-Encoder Networks.
papers.abstract: To achieve a more comprehensive understanding of cancer, novel computational methods are required for the integrative analysis of data from different molecular layers, such as genomics, transcriptomics, and epigenomics. Here, we present an innovative multi-omics integrative method that performs unsupervised representation learning, referred to as OMIDIENT: multiOMics Integration for cancer by DIrichlet auto-ENcoder neTworks. OMIDIENT provides a natural framework for modeling sparse and compositional latent representations by employing a deep generative model, where the latent space is distribu
papers.summary: OMIDIENT is a new deep learning method that integrates different types of molecular data (genomics, transcriptomics, epigenomics) to better understand cancer. It uses a Dirichlet auto-encoder to learn sparse and interpretable representations of the data without needing labels, which can help identify cancer subtypes and key molecular features.

papers.title: RankVar: machine learning-based variant ranking and reinterpretation for rare genetic diseases.
papers.abstract: Prior biological knowledge and phenotype information can help identify disease genes from whole genome/exome sequencing studies, but how best to incorporate external knowledge with variant data remains challenging. We developed a machine learning algorithm called RankVar to prioritize causative variants for rare diseases, based on clinical notes and genome/exome sequencing profiles.
papers.summary: RankVar is a new machine learning tool that prioritizes disease-causing genetic variants by combining clinical notes with genome/exome sequencing data. It helps researchers and clinicians more efficiently identify the causative mutations behind rare genetic diseases.

papers.title: Somatic mobility of transposons is explosive and shaped by distinct integration biases in Arabidopsis thaliana.
papers.abstract: The movement of transposable elements (TEs) in somatic cells generates genetic and phenotypic diversity across the soma of individual organisms. This process is especially important in plants that display a sessile lifestyle, can regenerate from somatic cells, and continuously establish their germline from somatic stem cells. Yet, the scale and integration biases by which TEs may shape the plant soma remain unknown.
papers.summary: This study reveals that transposable elements in Arabidopsis thaliana undergo explosive somatic movement, generating extensive genetic diversity within individual plants. The integration of these elements is not random but follows distinct biases that shape the plant's somatic genome. These findings highlight the significant role of somatic TE mobility in plant biology, particularly given plants' ability to regenerate from somatic cells.

papers.title: METEOR: a data-adaptive Mendelian randomization method for powerful detection of shared and specific exposures underlying multiple outcomes.
papers.abstract: Accurate identification of causal exposures for multimorbidity can benefit the co-prevention and co-management of multiple-related outcomes. This goal can be conceptually addressed within a multi-outcome Mendelian randomization (MR) framework. However, existing multi-outcome MR methods suffer from restrictions on format and availability of data inputs, fail to account for the potential sample overlap, rely on pre-selected independent instrumental variables (IVs), and are unable to account for horizontal pleiotropy. Here, we propose METEOR, a novel MR method that jointly models one exposure and
papers.summary: METEOR is a new Mendelian randomization method that can simultaneously analyze multiple health outcomes to identify shared and specific causal exposures, even when study samples overlap. It overcomes key limitations of existing methods by being more flexible with data inputs and accounting for horizontal pleiotropy, making it a practical tool for studying multimorbidity.

papers.title: Protocol for constructing multi-ancestry polygenic models using S4-Multi.
papers.abstract: Here, we present a protocol to construct multi-ancestry polygenic models using S4-Multi and generate polygenic risk scores. We describe steps for preparing reference genotype files, formatting genome-wide association stud (GWAS) summary statistics, and selecting SNPs using S4_select. We then perform Bayesian shrinkage with S4_shrink and describe procedures for polygenic score generation and performance evaluation. This workflow supports single- and multi-ancestry analyses and uses linkage disequilibrium information to improve prediction accuracy. For complete details on the use and execution o
papers.summary: This protocol describes how to use the S4-Multi tool to build polygenic risk scores that work across multiple ancestral populations. It provides step-by-step instructions for preparing genetic data, selecting relevant SNPs, and evaluating prediction accuracy, with the key advantage being that it uses linkage disequilibrium information to improve performance in diverse populations.

papers.title: Recent advances in the detection and functional analysis of circRNAs with short-read RNA sequencing-based methods.
papers.abstract: Circular RNAs (circRNAs) were first identified approximately 50 years ago in pathogenic viroids as single-stranded, covalently closed RNA molecules. Initially considered by-products of splicing, circRNAs are now recognised as an important class of regulatory RNAs involved in microRNA sponging, RNA-protein interactions, and cellular pathways. Their closed-loop structure, generated through backsplicing, confers resistance to exonucleolytic degradation and contributes to their stability. Owing to their tissue- and disease-specific expression, circRNAs have emerged as promising biomarkers for canc
papers.summary: This review summarizes recent advances in detecting and functionally analyzing circular RNAs (circRNAs) using short-read RNA sequencing methods. It highlights how circRNAs, once considered splicing by-products, are now recognized as stable regulatory molecules involved in microRNA sponging and protein interactions, with potential as cancer biomarkers.

papers.title: MPOInt: An Epigenetics-Driven Workflow for Integrative Multi-Level Proteomics.
papers.abstract: Proteomics provides key insights into systems regulating cell behavior and can elucidate key epigenetic states influencing biological functions in health and disease. Integrating different modalities of proteomics (total, phospho, epi) allows for a comprehensive understanding of how systems change in response to a stimulus. A current limitation is that these data are often captured individually with a limited scope of how systems regulating biological processes interact. Integrative analyses are essential to accurately depict biological states but often require custom, time-consuming bioinform
papers.summary: This paper presents MPOInt, a new computational workflow that integrates different types of proteomics data (total, phospho, and epigenetic) to provide a more complete picture of how biological systems respond to stimuli. The tool addresses the common problem of analyzing these data types separately, which limits understanding of how regulatory systems interact. MPOInt enables faster and more comprehensive integrative analyses without requiring extensive custom bioinformatics.

papers.title: Identification and functional characterization of regulatory variants in DPP9 associated with COVID-19 severity.
papers.abstract: Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection leads to a wide-range of clinical outcomes, which have been extensively studied through genome-wide association studies (GWAS).
papers.summary: This study identifies specific genetic variants in the DPP9 gene that are associated with severe COVID-19 outcomes, and shows that these variants affect gene regulation. The findings help explain how certain individuals may be genetically predisposed to more severe disease, providing a mechanistic link between GWAS signals and COVID-19 severity.

papers.title: An interpretable deep learning framework uncovers features governing CRISPR-Cas9 genome-editing efficiency.
papers.abstract: CRISPR-Cas9 genome-editing efficiency is strongly influenced by the sequence composition and positional context of single-guide RNAs (sgRNAs). Although numerous deep learning-based models have been developed to predict Cas9 efficiency from sgRNA sequences, most operate as black boxes, offering limited insight into the sequence determinants underlying Cas9 activity. In addition, previous studies often overlook how the positional context of sequence motifs within sgRNAs influences their effects on Cas9 binding or cleavage.
papers.summary: This paper presents an interpretable deep learning model that not only predicts CRISPR-Cas9 editing efficiency from sgRNA sequences but also reveals which sequence features and their positions within the sgRNA are most important for activity, moving beyond black-box predictions. The model identifies key sequence motifs and their positional context that govern Cas9 binding and cleavage, providing mechanistic insights into genome editing efficiency.

papers.title: DNA methylation clock in bull sperm cells reveals the epigenetic aging characteristics and impact on fertility.
papers.abstract: Aging is a crucial factor influencing semen quality and fertility in bulls, with potential implications for reproductive management and genetic improvement programs. Understanding the molecular mechanisms underlying sperm cell aging is essential for assessing reproductive potential and improving livestock productivity.
papers.journal: Journal of animal science and biotechnology
papers.summary: This study develops a DNA methylation clock for bull sperm cells, revealing epigenetic aging patterns that correlate with fertility decline. The findings provide a molecular tool to assess reproductive potential and improve livestock breeding programs.

papers.title: Small Structural Variations, Large Functional Consequences: Comparative Analysis Reveals Structural Control of Ubiquitylation Site Selection by BRCA1/BARD1.
papers.abstract: BRCA1/BARD1 is a chromatin-associated E3 ubiquitin ligase that ubiquitylates histone H2A to coordinate DNA damage repair, transcriptional repression, and genome stability. In Caenorhabditis elegans (C. elegans) , the orthologous BRC-1/BRD-1 com-plex performs analogous functions but exhibits structural variation, most notably through an additional 11-residue loop in BRD-1 that is absent from human BARD1. Prior exper-iments indicate this worm-specific insertion promotes nucleosome engagement and may alter the preferred lysine target for ubiquitylation. Here, we provide a cross-species comparison
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study compares the E3 ubiquitin ligase BRCA1/BARD1 in humans and C. elegans, finding that a small structural variation—an 11-residue loop in the worm BRD-1—alters which lysine residue on histone H2A gets ubiquitylated. This demonstrates how subtle structural differences can have large functional consequences for chromatin regulation and genome stability.

papers.title: Decoding Viral Dark Matter: Metagenomic Prokaryotic Virus Characterization With Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny.
papers.abstract: Viral metagenomics is an increasingly powerful tool for understanding the function and structure of viruses across the diverse environments of our planet. However, decoding the functional potential of prokaryotic viral metagenomes is extremely challenging. Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny are complementary open-source prokaryotic viral genome annotation tools that utilize a variety of bioinformatics approaches to maximally annotate viral metagenomes. This article describes a protocol for installing and running these tools on a viral metagenomic dataset, followed by visualization of annotations us
papers.summary: This protocol describes three complementary open-source tools (Pharokka, Phold, and Phynteny) for annotating prokaryotic viral genomes from metagenomic data, addressing the challenge of decoding viral 'dark matter'. The authors provide a step-by-step guide for installing and running these tools on viral metagenomic datasets, including visualization of annotations.

papers.title: DeepMethylation: A deep learning framework for tissue-specific DNA methylation prediction and functional variant annotation.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation is a key epigenetic modification that regulates gene expression and plays a vital role in cell differentiation, development, and tumorigenesis. However, large-scale experimental profiling of genome-wide DNA methylation remains time-consuming and limited in coverage. We present DeepMethylation, a deep learning framework that integrates DNA sequence and tissue-specific epigenomic features to predict CpG methylation status across the genome. DeepMethylation achieves state-of-the-art performance (average AUROC 0.909) across tissues, accurately imputes methylation beyond array-cover
papers.summary: DeepMethylation is a new deep learning tool that predicts DNA methylation patterns across different tissues by combining DNA sequence information with tissue-specific epigenomic features. It achieves high accuracy (average AUROC 0.909) and can impute methylation levels beyond standard array coverage, offering a computational alternative to costly experimental profiling.

papers.title: Deep learning in tumour genomics: from multi-omics integration to precision oncology.
papers.abstract: Cancer remains a leading cause of death globally, with nearly 10 million deaths in 2020. Advances in genomic technologies have revolutionized cancer research, shifting focus towards precision medicine based on comprehensive tumour genomic profiling. Concurrently, deep learning (DL) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for complex biological data. This review critically assesses recent advances in DL applications for tumour genomics, emphasizing four key domains: DNA sequencing analysis for mutation detection, gene expression profiling for cancer subtype classification, methylation function predi
papers.summary: This review critically assesses recent advances in deep learning applications in tumour genomics, covering DNA mutation detection, gene expression profiling for cancer subtypes, and methylation analysis. It highlights how deep learning can integrate multi-omics data to advance precision oncology.

papers.title: A survey on forensic age estimation through DNA methylation: recent progress and future directions towards legal age estimation.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation (DNAm) has emerged as one of the most promising molecular biomarkers for age estimation (AE), offering a powerful complement to traditional anthropological and radiological methods. Its potential spans forensic, biomedical, and humanitarian domains, particularly in the identification of deceased individuals and the estimation of legal age in living persons. Despite its rapid development, the forensic implementation of DNAm-based AE models remains challenged by issues of validation, dataset diversity, and tissue-specific variability. This survey provides an up-to-date overview o
papers.summary: This review examines the use of DNA methylation as a biomarker for estimating chronological age in forensic contexts, highlighting its potential for identifying deceased individuals and determining legal age in living persons. The authors discuss current challenges including validation issues, lack of diverse datasets, and tissue-specific variability that hinder forensic implementation. The paper provides an overview of recent progress and outlines future directions needed for legal age estimation applications.

papers.title: Integrative single-cell and bulk transcriptomics analysis reveals cellular heterogeneity, immune microenvironment dynamics, and prognostic signatures in oral squamous cell carcinoma.
papers.abstract: Oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) represents a significant global health burden with heterogeneous clinical outcomes. Understanding the molecular and cellular complexity of OSCC is crucial for developing precision therapeutic strategies.
papers.summary: This study integrates single-cell and bulk transcriptomics to map cellular heterogeneity and immune microenvironment dynamics in oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC), identifying distinct cell subtypes and prognostic signatures. The findings provide a comprehensive molecular atlas that could inform precision therapeutic strategies for OSCC.

papers.title: Study Protocol for a Randomized Controlled Trial of Multisensory Early Oral Administration of Human Milk (M-MILK) for Very Preterm Infants: Enhancing Stress Regulation, Neurodevelopment, and Oral Feeding Skills.
papers.abstract: Background. Early life stress results in disrupted stress regulation, and less optimal neurodevelopment and oral feeding skills in very preterm infants. Despite these associations, there are few evidence-based interventions to help these fragile infants during critical stages of development. Purpose. This article describes an ongoing research protocol of a randomized controlled trial (RCT) to evaluate the multisensory early oral administration of human milk (M-MILK) as an early NICU intervention to improve stress regulation, support optimal neurodevelopment, and promote competent oral feeding
papers.summary: This paper describes a protocol for a randomized controlled trial testing whether early oral administration of human milk (M-MILK) can help very preterm infants better regulate stress, develop neurologically, and feed orally. The study aims to address the lack of evidence-based interventions for these fragile infants in the NICU.

papers.title: Human gut flagellome profiling using FlaPro reveals TLR5-related phenotype-specific alterations in IBD.
papers.abstract: Flagellin, the structural protein of bacterial flagella, activates the innate immune receptor Toll-like receptor 5 (TLR5). However, the ability of different flagellins to bind and stimulate TLR5 varies widely, suggesting that the composition of an individual's flagellin repertoire, defined as flagellome, may influence host-microbiome interactions and inflammation. Here, we developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline for quantification and functional annotation of human gut flagellomes. Functional categories in FlaPro are derived from a machine learning model trained on experimentally characteri
papers.summary: The authors developed FlaPro, a computational pipeline that quantifies and functionally annotates the flagellin repertoire (flagellome) in the human gut microbiome. Using this tool, they found that the composition of flagellins, which activate the immune receptor TLR5, is altered in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) patients and correlates with specific clinical phenotypes.

papers.title: Nuclear mechanotransduction: tools for mechanical perturbation and chromatin characterization.
papers.abstract: Mechanical cues, ranging from matrix mechanical properties to dynamic mechanical loading, can be transmitted via structural proteins and signaling molecules to the nucleus to reorganize nuclear architecture and modulate chromatin accessibility. This mechanical regulation plays an important role in tissue regeneration and disease development. To gain deeper insights into the mechanical regulation of chromatin organization, it is essential to develop technologies that can apply mechanical inputs and characterize the resulting changes in nuclear structure and chromatin organization. Here, we revi
papers.summary: This review discusses how mechanical cues from the environment are transmitted to the nucleus to reorganize chromatin and influence gene expression, playing key roles in tissue regeneration and disease. It highlights the need for new technologies that can apply mechanical forces and measure the resulting changes in nuclear structure and chromatin organization.

papers.title: Epigenetic regulation of vertebrate limb development: from field specification to tissue morphogenesis.
papers.abstract: Vertebrate limb development provides an excellent model for understanding how epigenetic mechanisms coordinate the integration of positional information and temporal signals to generate complex three-dimensional structures. An increasing body of evidence shows that chromatin-based mechanisms, including DNA methylation, histone modifications, and higher-order chromatin organization, define the competence of progenitor cells to respond to morphogenetic signals with spatial and temporal precision during limb development. This review explores how epigenetic mechanisms orchestrate and regulate the
papers.summary: This review discusses how epigenetic mechanisms like DNA methylation, histone modifications, and chromatin organization control vertebrate limb development by coordinating positional and temporal signals. It highlights how these chromatin-based processes define progenitor cell competence to respond to morphogenetic cues with spatial and temporal precision.

papers.title: Integrative metagenomics and structural bioinformatics identify explainable gut microbial variants associated with Crohn's disease.
papers.abstract: Metagenomics has revealed disease-associated shifts in microbial taxa and functions in inflammatory bowel disease (IBD) patients. However, the role of genomic variation in gut commensals remains poorly understood. Here, we integrated metagenomic profiling, variant calling, and structural bioinformatics to identify disease-associated variants in the gut microbes. Crohn's disease (CD) and ulcerative colitis (UC) showed significant negative associations with Bacteroides uniformis, Bacteroides vulgatus, and Eubacterium rectale. These bacteria exhibited 190,712 single-nucleotide polymorphisms, incl
papers.summary: This study combines metagenomic profiling and structural bioinformatics to identify specific genetic variants in gut microbes associated with Crohn's disease, finding that certain bacterial species like Bacteroides uniformis are negatively associated with the disease. The research highlights how genomic variation in gut commensals may contribute to inflammatory bowel disease pathogenesis.

papers.title: Convergent GenX biodegradation by genomically designed and functionally screened synthetic bacterial consortia.
papers.abstract: GenX (hexafluoropropylene oxide dimer acid) has been introduced as a replacement for legacy polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFASs) compounds; however, accumulating evidence indicates this C6 ether-PFAS may exhibit comparable toxicity to PFOA while persisting in contaminated water supplies. Biological remediation offers genuine mineralization potential but has been constrained by the exceptional stability of carbon-fluorine bonds. Here, we demonstrate that synthetic bacterial consortia assembled through rational genomic design (GENIA) or microfluidic selection (Community G) achieve rapid GenX biode
papers.summary: Researchers developed synthetic bacterial consortia, either through rational genomic design or microfluidic selection, that can rapidly biodegrade GenX, a replacement PFAS compound with potential toxicity similar to PFOA. This work demonstrates a promising biological remediation approach for breaking down the stable carbon-fluorine bonds in these persistent environmental contaminants.

papers.title: From genomes to interventions: computational strategies transforming parasitology.
papers.abstract: In silico techniques have become essential in contemporary parasitology, offering swift, cost-effective, and scalable methods for exploring parasite biology, host-parasite interactions, drug resistance, the discovery of diagnostic markers, vaccine development, and the prioritization of therapeutic targets. Computational frameworks that encompass genomics, pangenomics, phylogenetics, transcriptomics, proteomics, structural biology, molecular docking, molecular dynamics simulations, artificial intelligence (AI), and immunoinformatics have collectively revolutionized parasite research. They enabl
papers.summary: This review discusses how computational methods like genomics, AI, and molecular simulations have transformed parasitology, enabling faster and cheaper discovery of drug targets, vaccines, and diagnostic markers. It highlights the integration of multiple in silico approaches to study parasite biology and host-parasite interactions.

papers.title: Comparative analysis of morphology and chloroplast genomes in endangered plant Madhuca pasquieri and two congeneric plants: revealing phylogenetic relationships.
papers.abstract: This study addresses the taxonomic ambiguity impeding the conservation of Madhuca pasquieri - an endangered tree species classified as vulnerable on the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) Red List and listed as a national second-class protected plant in China - by integrating morphological comparison with complete chloroplast genome analysis of three Madhuca species.
papers.summary: This study clarifies the taxonomic relationships of the endangered tree Madhuca pasquieri by comparing its morphology and complete chloroplast genome with two related species. The findings resolve previous classification uncertainties, which is crucial for guiding conservation efforts for this vulnerable species.

papers.title: A Region-Aware Structured Framework Improves Prediction of Gene Expression from DNA Methylation.
papers.abstract: DNA methylation is a key epigenetic modification that plays an important role in gene expression regulation and disease development. Inferring gene expression from DNA methylation provides a computational strategy for cross-omics integration and facilitates the exploration of regulatory relationships between epigenetic modifications and transcription. However, the regulatory effects of methylation on gene expression often exhibit complex characteristics, and methylation in different functional regions of a gene may follow distinct regulatory patterns. Existing methods typically lack the capaci
papers.summary: This paper presents a new computational framework that improves the prediction of gene expression from DNA methylation data by accounting for the distinct regulatory roles of methylation in different functional regions of genes. The method outperforms existing approaches by capturing region-specific patterns, enabling better cross-omics integration and understanding of epigenetic regulation.
